The Mamluks halted the Mongol advance at Ain Jalut, destroyed the last Crusader states, hosted a shadow Abbasid caliph in Cairo, profited from and taxed the Red Sea and Egyptian route of the spice trade, and fell to the Ottomans under Selim I.

title: The Neo-Babylonian Empire
summary: The empire ruled from Babylon between roughly 626 and 539 BCE, often called the Chaldean Empire after the dynasty that founded it. Nabopolassar broke free of Assyrian rule and, allied with the Medes, helped destroy Nineveh in 612 BCE; his son Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 605–562 BCE) made Babylon the monumental capital of the Near East, rebuilding it with the Ishtar Gate and a great ziggurat. The empire sacked Jerusalem in 587/586 BCE and deported much of its population, the event known as the Babylonian Captivity. It fell to Cyrus the Great's Persians in 539 BCE under its last king, Nabonidus.

title: The Phoenicians
summary: The Phoenicians were the Semitic-speaking Canaanite city-states of the Levantine coast — chiefly Tyre, Sidon, Byblos, and Arwad — who flourished from roughly 1200 to 300 BCE, especially in the centuries after the Bronze Age Collapse. They were the master maritime traders and shipbuilders of the Mediterranean, planting trading posts and colonies from Cyprus to the Atlantic, the greatest of them Carthage. They were never a unified empire but a network of independent cities, usually under the overlordship of Assyria, then Babylon, then Persia, whose navy they supplied. Their consonantal script reshaped writing across the Mediterranean, and their purple dye gave them their outside name.

title: The Safavid Empire
summary: The Iranian empire that ruled from 1501 to 1736 CE — founded by the teenage Shah Ismail I, who seized Tabriz and proclaimed himself shah at the head of the Qizilbash, the Turkmen tribal cavalry sworn to a militant Sufi order from Ardabil. The Safavids made Twelver Shi'a Islam the state religion of Iran, a transformation that has lasted into the present and that set them against the Sunni Ottoman Empire to the west and the Uzbeks to the east. Crushed by Ottoman firearms at Chaldiran in 1514, the dynasty reached its height under Shah Abbas I, who rebuilt the army, made Isfahan a celebrated capital, and tied the empire to the silk trade, before the state fell to Afghan invaders in 1722 and finally to Nadir Shah in 1736.

title: The Sasanian Empire
summary: The last great Persian empire before Islam, ruling Iran and Mesopotamia from 224 to 651 CE. Founded by Ardashir I, who overthrew the Parthians, the Sasanians made Zoroastrianism a state religion served by a powerful priesthood, built a capital at Ctesiphon, and fought Rome and then the Byzantine Empire for four centuries over the Near East. They reached their height under Khosrow I and Khosrow II, exhausted themselves in a final ruinous war with Byzantium, and were conquered by Arab Muslim armies in the 630s and 640s; the last shah, Yazdegerd III, was killed in 651. Persian administrative, artistic, and courtly forms then deeply shaped Islamic civilization.

title: The Seljuk Empire
summary: The Great Seljuk Empire (c. 1037–1194 CE) was a Sunni Muslim empire of the Seljuk Turks, a clan of Oghuz Turkic origin, that came to rule Iran, Iraq, and much of the Near East. Its leader Tughril Beg entered Baghdad in 1055 and took the title sultan, holding real power as protector of the Sunni Abbasid caliph, who kept religious authority. Under the great vizier Nizam al-Mulk it built a network of madrasas and a Persianized administration; at Manzikert in 1071 Alp Arslan defeated the Byzantine emperor and opened Anatolia to Turkic settlement. The Great Seljuk line in Iran was ended by the Khwarazmians in 1194, while successor states such as the Sultanate of Rum in Anatolia lasted into the age of the Mongols.

title: The Umayyad Caliphate
summary: The first hereditary Islamic dynasty, ruling from 661 to 750 CE with its capital at Damascus — founded by Mu'awiya after the civil war that followed the killing of the caliph Ali. The Umayyads presided over the great Arab-Muslim conquests, carrying their armies across North Africa, into Iberia in 711, and to the edges of India and Central Asia, building one of the largest empires the world had yet seen. They made Arabic the language of government, struck the first distinctly Islamic coinage, and raised the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem and the Great Mosque of Damascus. They ruled a vast non-Arab subject population, with a growing body of non-Arab converts, while privileging Arabs, and the grievances this bred, together with Shia and Kharijite opposition, helped bring the Abbasid Revolution that overthrew them in 750.

title: Zoroastrianism
summary: One of the world's oldest living religions, taught by the Iranian prophet Zarathustra at a date scholars cannot fix. It worships Ahura Mazda, the Wise Lord, and frames existence as a moral struggle between truth and the lie, in which each person must choose. Closely tied to Iranian kingship and above all to the Sasanian Empire before the Arab-Muslim conquest reduced it to a minority, it survives among the Parsis of India and Iranian Zoroastrians, and scholars debate its disputed influence on Jewish, Christian, and Islamic ideas of heaven, hell, and a final judgment.

title: The Wahhabi Movement
summary: A Sunni revivalist movement that began in the Najd region of central Arabia in the eighteenth century, preaching a strict return to the absolute oneness of God and the practice of the earliest Muslims. Its founder, Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, condemned saint veneration, tomb visitation, and much popular devotion as idolatry. A pact with the House of Saud bound the movement to a dynasty that would build the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, from which, in the oil age, its influence spread worldwide.

title: The Young Turks
summary: The reformist and nationalist opposition that tried to save the late Ottoman Empire by remaking it. Its leading body, the Committee of Union and Progress, forced the restoration of the constitution in the 1908 revolution and seized full power in a 1913 coup. Beginning in a hopeful equality of all Ottoman subjects, the movement turned toward Turkish nationalism, governed under a wartime triumvirate, organized the genocide of the empire's Armenians, and brought the empire into the First World War, where it fell apart.

title: Zionism
summary: The modern Jewish national movement that arose in late-nineteenth-century Europe and sought a Jewish homeland in the ancient land of Israel. Born from the experience of persecution and from old religious ties to the land, it ran in several rival strands, drove waves of Jewish immigration to Ottoman and then British-ruled Palestine, and culminated in the 1948 founding of Israel. That founding is marked by Israelis as independence and by Palestinians as the Nakba, the catastrophe of their displacement, and the meaning of the term is contested to this day.

title: Mehmed II and the Fall of Constantinople
summary: The Ottoman capture of Constantinople on May 29, 1453, by Sultan Mehmed II — the conquest that ended the Eastern Roman, or Byzantine, Empire after more than a thousand years centered on Constantinople, made the city the seat of a new Islamic empire, and was read across Christian Europe as the close of one age and the start of another.

title: Ramadan
summary: The ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar, during which observant Muslims fast from dawn to sunset — abstaining from food, drink, and other appetites in daylight as one of the Five Pillars of Islam. Tied by tradition to the first revelation of the Quran, it is a yearly discipline of restraint, charity, and communal worship that moves through the seasons and is now observed across a global Muslim population of roughly two billion people, on every continent.

title: The Armenian Genocide
summary: The systematic deportation and mass killing of the Ottoman Empire's Armenian population by the ruling Committee of Union and Progress during the First World War — beginning in April 1915 and recognized by most historians as the first genocide of the twentieth century, with estimates of the dead ranging from roughly 600,000 to 1.5 million, many clustering near or above one million.

title: The Discovery of Middle Eastern Oil
summary: The early-twentieth-century discovery of vast petroleum reserves beneath Persia, Iraq, and the Arabian Peninsula — and how it remade parts of the region, from small Gulf sheikhdoms to older states such as Iran and Iraq, into the strategic center of the world economy.

title: The Founding of Israel
summary: The establishment of the State of Israel in 1948, set in motion by the 1947 United Nations recommendation to partition British-ruled Palestine and sealed by the war that followed. David Ben-Gurion declared independence on 14 May 1948; neighbouring Arab states invaded; the war ended with an Israeli state larger than the partition lines. The same event is remembered by Israelis as the War of Independence and by Palestinians as the Nakba, the catastrophe in which around 700,000 people fled or were expelled, a double meaning that has shaped the region ever since.

title: The Graeco-Arabic Translation Movement
summary: The sustained, court- and elite-sponsored effort, centered in Abbasid Baghdad from roughly the eighth to the tenth century, to translate Greek scientific and philosophical works (along with some Persian and Indian material) into Arabic — drawing especially on Syriac-speaking Christian translators, alongside Muslim, Jewish, and other scholars, enabled by cheap paper and Abbasid wealth, and feeding the scholarship later called the Islamic Golden Age, much of which returned to Latin Europe centuries afterward.

title: The Greco-Persian Wars
summary: A series of conflicts, fought roughly between 499 and 449 BCE, in which the Achaemenid Persian Empire tried twice to bring the Greek city-states of the Aegean under its control and failed — producing the Athenian victory at Marathon, the famous stand at Thermopylae, the naval triumph at Salamis, and the rise of Athens and its Delian League, while seeding a lasting Western myth of free Greeks against an Eastern despot that the historical record only partly supports.

title: The Reconquista
summary: The long, intermittent expansion of Christian kingdoms across the Iberian Peninsula at the expense of Muslim-ruled al-Andalus, conventionally dated 711 to 1492 — a process whose unity as a single, continuous religious 'reconquest' is largely a later construction, since medieval Iberian rulers routinely crossed religious lines, allied with the other faith, and fought their co-religionists. It ended with the fall of Granada in 1492, the same year as the expulsion of Spain's Jews and the first voyage of Columbus.

title: The Iranian Constitutional Revolution
summary: A movement between 1905 and 1911 that forced Iran's Qajar shah to grant a written constitution and an elected parliament, the Majles, drawing together merchants, reformist clergy, and Western-influenced intellectuals. It was among the first such constitutional movements in the Middle East and Asia. Royal counter-attack, foreign intervention, and a Russian ultimatum crushed the parliament by 1911, but it left a durable language of law and representation, and a still-contested debate about the role of clergy in Iranian politics that reached forward to 1979.

title: The Ottoman Tanzimat Reforms
summary: A program of sweeping state reform in the Ottoman Empire between 1839 and 1876, aimed at centralizing the government, modernizing the army, law, taxation, and schooling, and binding a vast multi-religious population together by promising every subject equal protection. Opened by the Gulhane Edict of 1839 and extended by the 1856 Reform Edict, the Tanzimat reshaped the Ottoman state, unsettled the old Muslim-dominant order, and ended in the empire's first constitution in 1876.

title: The Suez Crisis
summary: The 1956 war over the Suez Canal, in which Britain, France, and Israel secretly colluded to attack Egypt, seize the canal zone, and undo Nasser's nationalization — a military success that became a political catastrophe and exposed Britain and France as no longer first-rank world powers.

title: The Sunni-Shia Split
summary: The major division within Islam, which began as a dispute over who should lead the Muslim community after the Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 CE. Sunnis came to accept the succession of caliphs chosen by the community's leading men, beginning with Abu Bakr. Shia Muslims held that leadership belonged to the Prophet's family, beginning with his cousin and son-in-law Ali. The disagreement deepened through civil war, Ali's caliphate and assassination, and above all the killing of his son Husayn at Karbala in 680, the event that came to define Shia identity. The full split was gradual, unfolding over centuries into distinct schools of law, theology, and devotion, and the relationship between the two branches has varied widely ever since.

title: Al-Biruni
summary: The Khwarezmian polymath who measured the Earth's size with a single mountain and a careful angle, and who travelled into India in the entourage of a conqueror only to produce a calm, comparative study of Hindu religion, science, and custom that many modern scholars treat as an early model of cross-cultural inquiry — a thinker whose central discipline was distinguishing what he had verified from what he had merely been told.

title: Al-Farabi
summary: The tenth-century philosopher whom the Islamic tradition honored as 'the Second Teacher' — second only to Aristotle — for systematizing Greek logic in Arabic and working within the tradition that read Plato and Aristotle as fundamentally in harmony. His emanationist metaphysics and his Platonic vision of a virtuous city led by a ruler who joins philosophical understanding to prophetic imagination set the agenda for Islamic philosophy, shaping Ibn Sina directly and reaching, through translation, into Jewish and Christian thought.

title: Al-Ghazali
summary: The Persian jurist, theologian, and mystic who, at the height of his fame, abandoned a prestigious teaching post in Baghdad for a decade of withdrawal — and whose two greatest works, a sharp critique of the Aristotelian philosophers and a vast synthesis of law with Sufi spirituality, reshaped mainstream Sunni Islam, making mysticism respectable and setting the terms on which faith and reason would be argued for centuries.

title: Al-Razi (Rhazes)
summary: The Persian physician of ninth- and tenth-century Rayy and Baghdad, Latinized as Rhazes, who ran hospitals, kept careful records of his patients, and wrote one of the earliest clear clinical accounts distinguishing smallpox from measles. His vast medical compendium, the Hawi or Continens, carried his case-based, observational style into the European universities, while his willingness to test Galen against his own findings made him one of the medieval world's sharpest clinical empiricists — and attacks on prophecy attributed to him by hostile opponents made him one of its most controversial figures.

title: Averroes / Ibn Rushd
summary: The Cordoban jurist, physician, and philosopher who spent his life trying to recover what he saw as the authentic Aristotle from the Neoplatonist overlay he believed earlier Muslim thinkers had added, and who answered al-Ghazali's attack on philosophy with a defense of reason as a path to the same truth as revelation — a body of work whose Latin and Hebrew afterlife was far larger than its immediate Islamic philosophical reception, though recent scholarship shows his works did not simply vanish from Muslim intellectual life.

title: Ataturk and the Founding of the Turkish Republic
summary: The Ottoman army officer who led the Turkish War of Independence, abolished the sultanate and caliphate, and founded the Republic of Turkey in 1923 as its first president. Through sweeping top-down reforms he built a secular, Western-facing nation-state on the ruins of a multiethnic empire. His achievements and his authoritarian, assimilationist methods are both part of the record, and both are still argued over.

title: Cyrus the Great
summary: The Persian king who, in roughly two decades, rose from a subject people to overthrow the Medes, Lydians, and Babylonians and assembled the Achaemenid Empire — the largest the world had yet seen — governing it through a pragmatic policy of restoring local cults and ruling through local elites that the Hebrew Bible praised and that a clay cylinder recorded, a record later, and contestedly, called the first charter of human rights.

title: Darius the Great
summary: The Achaemenid king who seized the Persian throne in 522 BCE amid a violent succession crisis and then reorganized the largest empire the world had yet seen -- dividing it into provinces with fixed tribute, standardizing coinage, building the Royal Road and the ceremonial capital at Persepolis -- while leaving a cliff-carved inscription that is both his official self-defense and a key that later unlocked cuneiform.

title: Euclid
summary: The Greek mathematician who, working in Alexandria around 300 BCE, compiled and organized the geometry of his predecessors into the 'Elements' — thirteen books that derived hundreds of theorems from a handful of definitions and axioms, founding the model of deductive proof and serving as the most successful textbook in history for roughly two thousand years, even though almost nothing reliable is known about the man himself.

title: Gamal Abdel Nasser
summary: The dominant Arab political figure of the mid-twentieth century, an Egyptian army officer who helped overthrow the monarchy in 1952 and ruled as president from 1956 until his death in 1970. He fused secular Arab nationalism, anti-imperialism, Arab socialism, and Cold War non-alignment into a creed later called Nasserism, nationalized the Suez Canal in 1956 and turned a military defeat into a vast political triumph, championed land reform and industrialization at home, and led the Non-Aligned Movement abroad. His prestige was shattered by the catastrophic defeat of the 1967 Six-Day War, and he governed a one-party state with a powerful secret police that jailed its critics, leaving a contested legacy of real gains for ordinary Egyptians alongside lasting authoritarian costs.

title: Ibn al-Haytham / Alhazen
summary: The mathematician and astronomer of Basra and Cairo, Latinized as Alhazen, who helped found the medieval science of mathematical and experimental optics. His Book of Optics (Kitab al-Manazir), written under the Fatimid caliphate around the 1010s, argued that vision works by light entering the eye from objects rather than by rays the eye sends out, and built that argument on systematic experiment and geometry. Translated into Latin as Perspectiva, the work shaped Roger Bacon, Witelo, and eventually Kepler, and his insistence on testing claims against controlled observation has led many modern writers to call him an early pioneer of the experimental method.

title: Jesus of Nazareth
summary: A Jewish teacher and healer from Roman-ruled Galilee who proclaimed the coming kingdom of God, taught in parables, gathered followers, and was crucified in Jerusalem under the prefect Pontius Pilate around 30–33 CE. After his death his followers proclaimed that God had raised him from the dead, and that proclamation launched the movement that became Christianity, the largest religion in the world. For roughly 2.4 billion Christians he is the incarnate Son of God and risen Lord; in Islam he is Isa, a revered prophet and the Messiah born of the Virgin Mary; and he remains one of the most studied figures in history, where the historical man and the Christ of faith must be carefully held apart.

title: Maimonides
summary: The preeminent medieval Jewish philosopher, legal scholar, and physician, who twice reorganized his tradition from inside the Islamic world: once by gathering the whole of Jewish law into a single clear code, the Mishneh Torah, and once by trying to square Aristotle's philosophy with the Hebrew Bible in The Guide for the Perplexed, a project so bold it provoked book-burnings among his own people even as it made him, in the tradition's own phrase, the one of whom 'from Moses to Moses there was none like Moses.'

title: Omar Khayyam
summary: The Persian mathematician and astronomer from Nishapur who gave medieval algebra its high point — a systematic classification and geometric solution of cubic equations using intersecting conic sections — and who led the reform of the Persian calendar under the Seljuks, yet who is known in the West chiefly as the poet of the Rubaiyat, a fame that rests on quatrains of uncertain attribution and on Edward FitzGerald's loose 1859 English version.

title: Ptolemy
summary: The Greek-speaking mathematician of Roman Alexandria who, around 150 CE, built the most accurate model of the heavens the ancient world produced — an Earth-centered system of circles upon circles that predicted where the planets would appear and held that authority for roughly fourteen centuries until Copernicus, Kepler, and Galileo overturned it; almost nothing is known of the man, so his career is read entirely through the works that bear his name.

title: Roxelana / Hurrem Sultan
summary: An enslaved woman from Ruthenia who became the freed, legally married wife of Suleiman the Magnificent at the height of the Ottoman Empire — she broke two long-standing customs of the dynasty, helped open the era later called the Sultanate of Women, corresponded with foreign kings, and funded major public works in Istanbul, while a hostile contemporary and Western tradition reduced her to a scheming concubine.

title: Rumi
summary: The 13th-century Persian Sufi poet, born in the eastern Islamic world and settled in Konya in Anatolia, who began as a respected Muslim scholar and jurist and, after his transforming friendship with the wandering mystic Shams of Tabriz, became the ecstatic poet of divine love whose vast Masnavi and lyric Divan made him one of the most widely read poets in any language — and whose order inspired the whirling sema ceremony of the Mevlevi dervishes.

title: Saladin
summary: The Kurdish-born founder of the Ayyubid dynasty who ended the Shia Fatimid caliphate in Egypt, united Egypt and Syria, crushed the Crusader army at Hattin in 1187, and recaptured Jerusalem for Islam eighty-eight years after the First Crusade had taken it — and whose comparatively restrained conduct toward the conquered turned him, in later Western and Muslim memory alike, into the archetype of the chivalrous, magnanimous foe.

title: Theodor Herzl
summary: The Austro-Hungarian Jewish journalist and playwright who turned scattered hopes for a Jewish homeland into an organized political movement. Born in Budapest and working in Vienna, he was an assimilated, secular Jew who came to believe that assimilation had failed and that no amount of legal equality would protect Jews from European hatred. His 1896 pamphlet Der Judenstaat argued that the Jews were a nation needing a state, and in 1897 he founded the World Zionist Organization at the First Zionist Congress in Basel. He spent his last years in tireless diplomacy seeking a charter for Jewish settlement, mostly without success, and died in 1904 at forty-four, decades before the state his movement pointed toward came into being. Revered by Israelis as the visionary founder, he is also faulted by critics for a plan that paid little attention to the Arabs already living in Palestine.

title: Suleiman the Magnificent
summary: The tenth Ottoman sultan, who reigned from 1520 to 1566, the longest of any Ottoman ruler, and presided over the empire at its height — he took Belgrade, Rhodes, and Hungary, besieged Vienna, dominated the Mediterranean, systematized the secular law that earned him the name 'the Lawgiver,' and patronized a golden age of Ottoman art and architecture, while the same reign was marked by the brutal logic of dynastic succession that led him to order the deaths of two of his own sons and his closest friend.

title: Al-Andalus
summary: The Muslim-ruled lands of the Iberian Peninsula between the conquest of 711 and the fall of Granada in 1492 — a medieval civilization of great cities, libraries, irrigation, philosophy, and poetry, where Muslims, Christians, and Jews lived together in an arrangement that was productive and unequal at once, and whose memory still serves as a contested symbol of coexistence lost.

title: Baghdad
summary: The city founded in 762 CE by the Abbasid caliph al-Mansur on the Tigris as a planned circular capital, which grew into one of the largest and richest cities in the medieval world and the center of the Islamic Golden Age, before the Mongol sack of 1258 and centuries as a provincial town — and which is now the capital of Iraq.

title: Cairo
summary: The city a Shia dynasty founded in 969 CE beside the Nile and named al-Qahira, 'the Victorious' — which grew into the largest and richest metropolis of the medieval Islamic world, home to the al-Azhar mosque and its thousand-year center of learning, and is today the capital of Egypt and the largest city in the Arab world.

title: Cordoba
summary: The city on the Guadalquivir in southern Spain that rose from a Roman provincial capital to become, as Qurtuba, the capital of Muslim al-Andalus and one of the largest and most learned cities of the tenth-century world — whose Great Mosque, later turned into a cathedral, still stands as the central monument and the central argument about who the city belongs to.

title: Persepolis
period: c. 518 BCE – 330 BCE (Achaemenid); ruin and rediscovery to the present
summary: The ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Persian Empire in southwestern Iran, built from around 518 BCE and burned by Alexander the Great in 330 BCE. Its carved reliefs of subject peoples bringing gifts to the king turned an empire of two dozen nations into a single sculptural image of order, and its ruins became a charged symbol of the ancient Persian past and modern Iranian identity.

title: Palmyra
period: c. 1st century BCE – 3rd century CE (height); ruins endure to the present
summary: The oasis caravan city in the Syrian desert that grew rich brokering trade between the Roman Mediterranean and Persia, India, and China, briefly broke away under Queen Zenobia, and survived as a magnificent ruin until parts of it were deliberately destroyed in 2015.

title: Damascus
summary: An oasis city watered by the Barada river and among the oldest continuously inhabited places on earth — ruled in turn by Aramaeans, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, and Romans, made the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate that ruled from Spain to India, and today the contested capital of Syria.

title: Petra
period: c. 300 BCE – 6th century CE (Nabataean, Roman, Byzantine); Western reintroduction 1812
summary: The rock-cut capital of the Nabataean kingdom in southern Jordan, where desert nomads grew rich controlling the incense caravan trade and engineered a city to thrive in a place with almost no water — abandoned after Rome and a shifting trade map drained it, and famous in the modern West as a city that was "lost" and then "found."

title: The Alhambra
summary: The red-walled palace-fortress on the hill above Granada, built by the Nasrid dynasty in the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries as the last Muslim kingdom in Western Europe contracted toward its final stronghold — the supreme surviving work of late Andalusi art, and the place where al-Andalus ended when Granada surrendered to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492.

title: The Dome of the Rock
summary: The golden-domed Umayyad shrine on the walled platform that Muslims call the Haram al-Sharif and Jews call the Temple Mount — one of the oldest surviving works of Islamic architecture, raised over a rock claimed as sacred by three faiths and contested by two peoples.

title: The Great Mosque of Mecca and the Hajj
summary: The Sacred Mosque (al-Masjid al-Haram) in Mecca, commonly described as the largest mosque in the world, and the pilgrimage it gathers — the Hajj, one of the Five Pillars of Islam, a duty owed once in a lifetime by every able and able-to-afford Muslim. At its center stands the Kaaba, the cube toward which Muslims everywhere turn to pray, and around it each year flows one of the largest recurring gatherings of humanity on earth, a coordinated rite of consecration, circling, and standing that erases rank and binds a global community for a few sacred days.

title: The Hagia Sophia
summary: The great domed church of Holy Wisdom in Constantinople, completed in 537 CE under the emperor Justinian I — for most of nearly a thousand years the cathedral of the Eastern Orthodox world, interrupted by Latin Catholic rule after the Fourth Crusade, then an Ottoman mosque after 1453, a secular museum under the Turkish republic from the 1930s, and a working mosque again since 2020, making the single building a record of every power that has claimed the city.

title: The House of Wisdom
period: Abbasid period; institution uncertain, legendary destruction tied to 1258
summary: The Abbasid institution known as Bayt al-Hikma, tied in popular memory to a grand state academy of translators in ninth-century Baghdad — but which careful scholarship suggests was most likely a far more modest caliphal palace library and translation bureau, possibly tied especially to Persian administrative and astrological material. The entry is an anatomy of how a real but smaller institution becomes a civilizational myth, and why the smaller truth may be the more impressive one.

title: The Millet System
period: Ottoman communal governance, 15th–20th centuries; formal millet system chiefly 19th century
summary: The arrangement by which the Ottoman Empire governed its non-Muslim subjects as self-administering religious communities — chiefly Greek Orthodox, Armenian, and Jewish — each headed by its own clergy and answerable to the sultan for taxes, law, and order within the group. Neither modern toleration nor simple oppression, it was a hierarchical accommodation that granted communal autonomy and protection of worship while keeping non-Muslims legally subordinate. The tidy three-millet scheme often described as ancient was largely a nineteenth-century construction, and the system broke apart as nationalism turned religious communities into rival nations.

title: The Second Temple
summary: The sanctuary that stood in Jerusalem from roughly 516 BCE until Rome destroyed it in 70 CE — the center of Jewish sacrifice, priesthood, and pilgrimage, whose loss forced Judaism to remake itself around prayer, study, and law rather than a single holy building.

title: Euclid's Elements
summary: The geometry textbook written in Alexandria around 300 BCE that gave the world its most influential ancient model of the axiomatic method — truth built up by proof from a handful of stated assumptions — and remained the standard model of rigorous reasoning, and of geometry teaching, for more than two thousand years.

title: The Hadith
period: c. 632 CE – present (collection, criticism, and transmission)
summary: The vast body of reported words, deeds, and silent approvals of the Prophet Muhammad — transmitted through chains of named witnesses, sifted by an elaborate science of authentication, and gathered into canonical collections — that stands after the Quran as the second source of Islamic law and practice for mainstream Sunni and Shia Islam, though the accepted collections differ.

title: The Avesta
period: Old Avestan Gathas perhaps c. 1200 BCE; written down under the Sasanians, c. 5th–6th century CE
summary: The sacred scripture of Zoroastrianism, written in an otherwise-unrecorded ancient Iranian language called Avestan. Its oldest core, the Gathas, is a set of hymns traditionally attributed to the prophet Zarathustra and among the oldest religious poetry in any Indo-Iranian language. Carried orally by priests for many centuries and committed to writing only late, under the Sasanian Empire, the Avesta survives today as a fraction of a once-vast canon — most of it lost over the long decline of Zoroastrianism after the Arab conquest of Persia, the remainder preserved chiefly through Parsi and Iranian Zoroastrian manuscript lines.

title: The Balfour Declaration
summary: The single-sentence statement, about 67 words long, issued by the British government on 2 November 1917 as a letter from Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour to Lord Rothschild. It pledged British support for "a national home for the Jewish people" in Palestine while promising not to prejudice the "civil and religious rights" of the existing non-Jewish communities, then the large Arab majority. Its power lies in its calculated ambiguity: it never defined "national home," it named the Arab majority only by what they were not, and it clashed with other wartime British promises. Folded into the Mandate, it became a founding document for the State of Israel and a founding grievance in the Palestinian national story.

title: The Pyramids of Giza
summary: Three great stone pyramids on the Giza plateau outside modern Cairo, built during Egypt's Fourth Dynasty in the Old Kingdom, roughly 2600 to 2500 BCE. The largest, the Great Pyramid of Khufu, rose about 146 metres and was the tallest human-made structure on earth for nearly four thousand years. With the pyramids of Khafre and Menkaure, the Great Sphinx, queens' pyramids, and mortuary temples, they formed royal burial complexes meant to turn a dead king into a god. Built by a large organized workforce, not by enslaved Israelites or whip-driven slaves, they are the only surviving Wonder of the ancient world and the global emblem of Egypt.

title: The Shahnameh
summary: The national epic of Greater Iran, the "Book of Kings," composed by the poet Abolqasem Ferdowsi of Tus over roughly three decades and completed around 1010 CE. Running to some 50,000 rhyming couplets, it is among the longest epic poems ever written by a single author. It carries the mythical and legendary past of Iran from the creation of the world through some fifty reigns down to the Arab-Muslim conquest of the Sasanian Empire. Famous for using very little Arabic vocabulary, the poem is widely credited with helping preserve the Persian language and a distinct Iranian identity within the Islamic world, and it became the supreme subject of Persian miniature painting and oral storytelling.

title: The Sykes-Picot Agreement
summary: The secret 1916 understanding between Britain and France, with Imperial Russian assent, on how to divide the Arab provinces of the Ottoman Empire into spheres of influence and control after an expected Allied victory in the First World War. Negotiated mainly by Sir Mark Sykes and Francois Georges-Picot, it sketched a French northern zone, a British southern zone, and an international Palestine. The deal contradicted wartime British encouragement of Arab independence, and when the Bolsheviks published it in 1917 it became a lasting symbol of Western betrayal and of the colonial origins of the modern Middle East. Scholars caution that the popular claim that it drew today's borders is an oversimplification.

title: The Talmud
period: c. 200 CE (Mishnah) – c. 500–600 CE (Babylonian Talmud); ongoing study
summary: The vast compendium of Jewish law, lore, and debate that became the core text of rabbinic Judaism after the Hebrew Bible — built in two layers, the concise Mishnah redacted in Roman Palestine around 200 CE and the sprawling Gemara that argues over it, and compiled into two versions, the Jerusalem Talmud and the larger, more authoritative Babylonian Talmud completed in the academies of Sasanian Mesopotamia.

title: Maya Script
summary: The most fully developed writing system of the pre-Columbian Americas, and the only one known to record connected speech in full. Maya script mixes whole-word signs with phonetic syllable signs, recording the dynastic history, ritual, astronomy, and calendar of the Maya — a body of knowledge nearly erased by Spanish book-burning, then painstakingly read again in the twentieth century.

title: The Maya Calendar
summary: Not one calendar but a set of interlocking cycles — a 260-day ritual round, a 365-day solar year, and a linear day-count reaching back to a mythical creation — built on a base-20 place-value system and an independently developed sign for zero. It let Maya scribes fix any day in deep time, schedule the rituals of kingship, and write some of the most sophisticated astronomical tables of the pre-Columbian Americas.

title: Chichén Itzá
period: c. 600 – 1200/1250 CE (peak as a Maya city); a Wonder and tourist site today
summary: A great pre-Columbian Maya city in the northern Yucatán of Mexico, which rose in the Terminal Classic and dominated much of the northern lowlands between roughly 800 and 1100 CE before declining by about 1200 to 1250. Its monuments include El Castillo (the Temple of Kukulcán), the largest Mesoamerican ball court, the round Caracol, the Temple of the Warriors, and the Sacred Cenote into which offerings were thrown. Famous for a blend of Maya and central-Mexican styles whose origin scholars now debate, it remained a place of pilgrimage after its decline and is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a New 7 Wonders of the World.

title: Cozumel
period: c. 800 CE (Maya settlement; pilgrimage peak in the Postclassic)–present
summary: An island off the Caribbean coast of Mexico's Yucatan Peninsula that was one of the most important pilgrimage destinations in the Maya world, especially in the Postclassic period (roughly 1200–1500 CE). Maya travelers, by Spanish accounts often women hoping for safe childbirth, crossed the sea to consult an oracle of the goddess often identified with Ix Chel, associated with the moon, healing, childbirth, weaving, and divination. Cortes stopped here in 1519 at the start of the conquest of Mexico; disease and conquest later emptied the island. Today it is one of the world's busiest cruise-ship ports and a famous scuba-diving destination on the Mesoamerican Reef.

title: Cacao
period: c. 3300 BCE (early Upper Amazon use) – present
summary: The bitter bean of a tropical American tree (Theobroma cacao, 'food of the gods'), processed by Mesoamerican peoples into a spiced, frothy drink and used in Postclassic and contact-period Mesoamerica, by the Maya and the Aztecs, as money, tribute, and sacred substance — cacao crossed to Europe after the conquest, was sweetened into the chocolate the modern world eats, and now rests on a global cacao belt whose West African farms carry documented problems of poverty and child labor.

title: Coral Reefs
summary: A coral reef is a vast living structure built over thousands of years by colonies of tiny animals whose limestone skeletons accumulate into the most biodiverse habitat in the sea — a system that covers a tiny fraction of the ocean floor yet shelters perhaps a quarter of all marine species, shields coastlines, and feeds hundreds of millions, and that warming and acidifying seas have now made one of the most threatened ecosystems on Earth.

title: The Garifuna
summary: An Afro-Indigenous people of the Caribbean coast of Central America — chiefly Honduras, Belize, Guatemala, and Nicaragua, with a large diaspora in the United States. The Garifuna (or Garínagu) formed on the island of St. Vincent from the mixing of Indigenous Island Caribs with Africans, were deported by the British in 1797 after years of resistance, and carried across the sea an Indigenous language, a distinctive music and spirituality, and a way of life that UNESCO named a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity.

title: The Golden Age of Piracy
summary: The burst of intense maritime robbery, roughly the 1650s to 1730, that swept the Caribbean, the Atlantic, and the Indian Ocean. The entry treats piracy not as a world apart but as the violent shadow of empire — licensed in wartime, hunted in peace — and weighs the romantic pirate of fiction against the thinner, harder record.

title: British Honduras
summary: The colonial extraction settlement on the Caribbean coast of Central America that became modern Belize. Conjured out of a single tree, it began as a cluster of English logcutters, some with buccaneering pasts, felling logwood for textile dye, then shifted to mahogany cut by enslaved Africans deep in the forest. Claimed by Spain but never made a dense settler colony, formalized by Britain only in 1862, and contested by Guatemala to this day, it left behind an English-speaking, plural society wedged into Spanish-speaking Central America.

title: Hurricanes
summary: A hurricane is the ocean's great heat engine turned loose — a vast rotating storm that draws its power from warm tropical water and converts it into wind, rain, and the deadliest hazard of all, storm surge. The same force is called a hurricane in the Atlantic, a typhoon in the western Pacific, and a cyclone in the Indian Ocean. The word itself carries a history: it comes through Spanish from a Caribbean Indigenous name for these storms, later linked to the Maya storm god Huracán, and the Caribbean and Gulf it haunts are among the most hurricane-prone seas on Earth. Hurricanes have repeatedly reshaped the region's colonial economies, its naval history, and its patterns of migration, and a warming ocean is expected to make the strongest of them more intense.

title: The Thousand and One Nights
summary: A frame-tale collection of stories in Arabic, known in the West as "The Arabian Nights" (Arabic: Alf Layla wa-Layla). It has no single author, no fixed date, and no settled text. Instead it grew by accretion over many centuries, building on an older and now-lost Persian collection with possible Indian roots, an Arabic Baghdad layer set in the court of Harun al-Rashid, and a later Egyptian layer compiled in Cairo. The frame is famous: the betrayed king Shahryar kills a new wife each dawn until Shahrazad keeps herself alive by telling stories that break off unfinished at sunrise. The work was marginal in the classical Arabic tradition and became a global sensation only through Antoine Galland's French translation (1704–1717), which also introduced "Aladdin" and "Ali Baba," tales now thought to be absent from the core Arabic manuscripts.

title: Sharia
summary: In Muslim thought, sharia is God's eternal way for human life, known in full only to God; what people call sharia in practice is fiqh, the centuries of fallible human jurisprudence that tries to understand and derive it. Plural, revisable, and overwhelmingly concerned with worship and everyday conduct rather than the harsh punishments of Western caricature, it is drawn from the Quran and the Prophet's example through schools of law that disagree among themselves — and its meaning is fiercely contested in the modern age of nation-states.

title: The Chola Empire
summary: The Tamil dynasty of southern India that, at its height between the tenth and twelfth centuries, turned a river delta into the base of a rare thing in Indian history — a great sea power, whose navy crossed the Bay of Bengal to raid Southeast Asia and whose temples still stand.

title: The Delhi Sultanate
summary: The first major and enduring Muslim-ruled state in the Indian subcontinent (1206–1526), founded by a Turkic former slave-soldier and ruled by five successive dynasties from Delhi. It planted Persianate Islamic high culture across a Hindu-majority land, repelled repeated Mongol invasions that devastated the rest of Asia, and is read today in sharply opposed ways.

title: Hinduism
summary: Not a single religion with a founder, a creed, or a church, but a vast family of related Indian traditions that share a body of sacred texts and core ideas — duty, action and its fruits, rebirth, and release — while differing enormously in deity, practice, and philosophy. The very word is largely a modern, colonial-era umbrella; many practitioners call it Sanatana Dharma, the eternal way. It is now the world's third-largest religious tradition.

title: Jainism
period: c. 8th c. BCE (Parshvanatha tradition); Mahavira c. 6th–5th c. BCE – present
summary: One of the world's oldest living religions, born in the same ancient Indian ferment as Buddhism among the non-Vedic renouncers, and built around a single principle carried further than perhaps any other tradition has carried it: ahimsa, non-harm to every living being, down to the smallest. Its central teacher is Mahavira, by tradition the last of twenty-four ford-makers, though Jains hold the path itself to be eternal.

title: The Maratha Confederacy
summary: The Marathi-speaking power of the western Deccan that, rising under Shivaji against the Deccan sultanates and the Mughals, broke Mughal dominance in the eighteenth century and spread across much of India — only to lose, divided among rival houses, to the British East India Company by 1818.

title: The Maurya Empire
summary: The first state to bring most of the Indian subcontinent under a single rule — a centralized, bureaucratic empire whose greatest emperor, Ashoka, conquered a region called Kalinga, recorded his own remorse on rock and pillar, and helped turn Buddhism into a world religion.

title: Sikhism
summary: The religion founded in the Punjab by Guru Nanak (1469–1539) on the teaching that there is one formless God beyond every human division, and that caste, ritualism, and the Hindu–Muslim divide are beside the point — a truth lived through honest work, sharing with others, and devotion. Carried forward by a line of ten Gurus and then by a living scripture, the Guru Granth Sahib, and the initiated community of the Khalsa, it is today the world's fifth-largest organized religion.

title: The Bhakti Movement
summary: A loose, centuries-long family of devotional movements that reshaped Hinduism through vernacular song. Sung in everyday languages rather than Sanskrit, it placed personal love of God above ritual and priestly mediation, and often above caste, making the divine reachable by the low-caste, the illiterate, and women — even as it was also carried by temple worship and royal patronage. Traced from the Tamil south of roughly the sixth century, it spread across India over the next thousand years and remains the dominant mode of popular Hindu worship.

title: The Kushan Empire
summary: The crossroads empire of Central Asia and northern India — built by nomads turned kings, enriched by the Silk Road trade between Han China and Rome, and remembered above all as the patron that helped carry Buddhism north and east across Asia.

title: The Pala Empire
summary: The Buddhist dynasty of Bengal and Bihar that, as Buddhism faded across the rest of India, became its last great imperial patron — sustaining Nalanda, founding Vikramashila, and serving as the launch point from which Vajrayana Buddhism passed to Tibet.

title: The Ghaznavid Empire
summary: A Turkic slave-soldier dynasty that ruled from Ghazni in Afghanistan from about 977 to 1186 CE, founded by Sebuktigin and dominated by his son Mahmud of Ghazni. It became famous in two opposite ways: as the raiders who first carried sustained Muslim campaigns deep into northern India, and as one of the most brilliant patrons of Persian high culture. Mahmud's campaigns into the Indian plains, above all the sack of the temple of Somnath, planted Muslim rule in the Punjab and are remembered today in sharply contested ways.

title: The Satavahana Dynasty
period: c. 1st century BCE – early 3rd century CE
summary: The first major state of the Deccan, the great plateau of peninsular India, which bridged the gap between the Mauryan collapse and the later kingdoms and grew rich where its Deccan routes fed a booming trade between the Roman world and the East.

title: The Vijayanagara Empire
summary: The last great empire of medieval South India (c. 1336–1646), a vast and fabulously wealthy Hindu-ruled state that dominated the peninsula for over two centuries from its wonder-city on the Tungabhadra river. Grown rich on cotton, spices, diamonds, and imported war horses, it astonished foreign travelers before a coalition of Deccan sultanates broke it at the Battle of Talikota in 1565 and reduced its capital to the ruins now called Hampi.

title: Yoga
summary: An Indian spiritual discipline whose name means "to yoke" or "union" — for most of its long history a rigorous path for stilling the mind and freeing the self, not a form of exercise. Rooted in the ascetic and Upanishadic world of the first millennium BCE and given classical shape in Patanjali's Yoga Sutras, it taught liberation through ethics, breath, concentration, and meditation, with physical posture only one small part. The bending, stretching practice the modern world calls yoga took form mainly in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries and has become a global, largely secular wellness practice that has mostly shed its metaphysics.

title: The Bengal Famine of 1943
summary: The mass starvation of roughly two to three million people in British-ruled Bengal during 1943 and 1944, in one of the most economically important — yet deeply unequal — provinces of the British Empire, in the middle of the Second World War. No single drought explains it. Its causes were a tangle of wartime disruption, the Japanese conquest of Burma, scorched-earth defense measures, inflation, market collapse, and slow colonial relief — and the question of British and Churchill-era responsibility remains fiercely debated.

title: The Rebellion of 1857
summary: The vast uprising across northern India against the rule of the British East India Company, which began in May 1857 as a mutiny of the Company's Indian soldiers and swelled into a broad revolt of soldiers, peasants, and dispossessed elites. It was the bloodiest challenge the British ever faced in India, marked by atrocities on both sides, and the Company crushed it with great ferocity. Its defeat ended Company rule, transferred India to the direct government of the British Crown, ended the Mughal dynasty, and left a memory so contested that the event has no single agreed name.

title: The Bangladesh Liberation War
summary: The 1971 war in which East Pakistan broke away from West Pakistan to become independent Bangladesh — a country built on a single religion that tore in two over language and political power. After the Pakistani army's crackdown on 25 March 1971 unleashed mass killing, rape, and the flight of roughly ten million refugees into India, Bengali guerrillas and an Indian military intervention forced the Pakistani surrender on 16 December 1971. The death toll is bitterly contested, ranging from official Bangladeshi figures of three million to independent estimates in the hundreds of thousands.

title: Vasco da Gama's Voyage to India
summary: The first direct all-ocean route from western Europe to India around Africa, opened when the Portuguese captain Vasco da Gama rounded the Cape of Good Hope and reached Calicut on the Indian coast in 1498 — loosening the chain of Arab, Indian, and Venetian middlemen who had carried Asian goods to Europe, and opening the era of armed European seaborne empire in Asia.

title: Adi Shankara
summary: The South Indian philosopher who gave Advaita Vedanta its classical form and Hinduism one of its most influential metaphysical systems, arguing that only one reality, Brahman, truly exists, that the individual self is identical with it, and that the world of separate things is appearance. His commentaries on the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Brahma Sutras made Advaita Vedanta one of the most influential intellectual articulations of the tradition.

title: Akbar
summary: The third Mughal emperor, who inherited a shaky North Indian realm at thirteen and turned it into one of the great powers of the early-modern world. Uniquely among the Muslim rulers of India, he made religious conciliation a principle of state — abolishing the tax on non-Muslims, allying with Hindu Rajput clans, and setting the throne above every faith — while remaining a conquering, sometimes ruthless emperor whose armies sacked the fortress of Chittor.

title: Babur
summary: The Central Asian prince who lost his ancestral kingdom and won an empire instead — descended from both Timur and Genghis Khan, driven out of Samarkand by the Uzbeks, he seized Kabul, then conquered northern India at Panipat in 1526 and founded the Mughal dynasty. He also wrote the Baburnama, one of the most candid memoirs any conqueror has left.

title: Jawaharlal Nehru
summary: Independent India's first and longest-serving prime minister, who governed from 1947 to 1964 and, more than any other single figure, stamped the new nation as a secular, democratic, state-planned, and non-aligned republic — entrenching parliamentary rule and free elections, building industry through Five-Year Plans, and leading the bloc of nations that refused to choose sides in the Cold War, while leaving behind the contested record over Kashmir, the 1962 defeat by China, slow growth, and his daughter's later succession that frames India's arguments with itself to this day.

title: Kabir
summary: The weaver-poet traditionally remembered as a low-status julaha of Benares, dated to the 15th century, who became the most piercing voice of North India's sant tradition of devotion to a God without form. By tradition raised in a Muslim weaver family in a holy Hindu city, he mocked the externals of both religions in short, blunt, vernacular verses that have been claimed since by Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs alike. He wrote nothing down; his sayings survive in variant, contested streams, including the Sikh scripture, which makes the recovery of the historical Kabir genuinely uncertain.

title: Kalidasa
summary: The supreme poet and dramatist of classical Sanskrit, conventionally placed in the Gupta golden age of the fourth and fifth centuries CE, about whose life almost nothing certain is known — yet whose grace of language, beauty of simile, and fusion of human longing with the natural world set the standard for Indian literature ever after, and whose play The Recognition of Shakuntala, in its first European translation, astonished Goethe and the readers of the late eighteenth century.

title: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
summary: The Karachi-born, London-trained barrister who began his career as a champion of Hindu-Muslim unity and ended it as the founder of Pakistan, the first modern state created expressly as a homeland for Muslims — winning it not through mass agitation but through constitutional argument and the two-nation theory, and serving barely a year as its first governor-general before tuberculosis killed him in 1948, leaving the question of whether he wanted a secular state or a religious one to be fought over ever since.

title: Rabindranath Tagore
summary: The Bengali poet, songwriter, novelist, painter, and educator who in 1913 became the first non-European to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, on the strength of his own spare English recasting of selected Bengali devotional poems — and who then spent the rest of his life resisting every box the world tried to put him in, lionized in the West as a mystic sage of a 'spiritual East' he distrusted, while at home he remade Bengali letters and music and wrote the words and music for the national anthems of two countries.

title: Ranjit Singh
summary: The one-eyed warlord who fused a dozen rival Sikh war-bands into a single kingdom and gave the Sikhs, for the first and only time, an empire of their own. Ranjit Singh (1780–1839), the Lion of Punjab, captured Lahore in 1799 and was proclaimed Maharaja in 1801, built a state across the Punjab, Kashmir, and Peshawar, and is remembered for ruling Sikhs, Hindus, and Muslims with unusual even-handedness. The empire collapsed within a decade of his death and was annexed by the British.

title: Srinivasa Ramanujan
summary: The poor, almost entirely self-taught South Indian clerk who, with little formal training, produced mathematics of such strange depth that it still feels less discovered than received — who sent his theorems to Cambridge, worked with G.H. Hardy, was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and died at 32, the great modern emblem of raw, untaught mathematical genius.

title: Tipu Sultan
summary: Ruler of Mysore who fought the British East India Company harder, and with more modern means, than almost any Indian prince of his age. Tipu Sultan (1750–1799), the Tiger of Mysore, modernized his army, expanded Mysore's iron-cased war rockets, sought a French alliance, and died defending his capital at Seringapatam in 1799. He is remembered both as an early resister of British rule and, by critics, for documented violence and forced conversions against some Hindu and Christian communities, and he remains fiercely contested in India today.

title: Amritsar
summary: The holiest city of the Sikhs, built around a sacred pool and the Golden Temple — a low shrine open on all four sides to people of every caste and faith — which in the twentieth century became the stage for two of the deepest wounds in modern Indian history: the 1919 Jallianwala Bagh massacre and the 1984 army assault on the temple complex.

title: Bollywood
summary: Bollywood is the Hindi-language commercial film industry of Mumbai (Bombay), one branch of the world's largest national cinema. From the silent feature of 1913 through the song-and-dance 'masala' blockbuster of today, it became one of the most-watched storytelling machines on earth and a binding imagination for a subcontinent of many languages and faiths. The nickname blends Bombay with Hollywood, and many in the industry resent it: far from a Hollywood imitation, Mumbai's Hindi cinema grew from India's own theatrical, musical, and literary traditions as much as from global film. The films are long, star-driven, and built on songs that the actors mime to playback singers. Those songs are the popular music of much of South Asia. At its center sits a rare space of Hindu-Muslim collaboration and a shared, Urdu-inflected 'Hindustani' culture, now negotiating a louder religious nationalism, debates over star dynasties, and the pull of a global diaspora audience.

title: Cricket
summary: Cricket is a bat-and-ball game between two teams of eleven, codified in eighteenth-century England and carried across the British Empire as a tool of imperial culture meant to teach colonial subjects an idealized set of 'English' values. The colonized embraced it, mastered it, and turned it around: beating England at its own game became a form of dignity and self-assertion. Today the sport's heartland is the Indian subcontinent, where it is a near-religion, India-Pakistan matches draw hundreds of millions, and the money and power have shifted decisively from London to India. Cricket is the rare imperial inheritance the former subjects made more their own than the rulers ever were.

title: Gandhara
summary: The ancient crossroads region around the Peshawar valley and Swat, in today's northwestern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan, where Greek and Indian worlds fused under Kushan rule to produce some of the first human images of the Buddha — an art that traveled the Silk Road and shaped Buddhist sculpture across half of Asia.

title: Hampi
summary: The ruined capital of the Vijayanagara Empire, scattered across a vast field of granite boulders on the Tungabhadra river in Karnataka, southern India. For two centuries one of the richest cities on earth, it was sacked over months after the Battle of Talikota in 1565 and never rebuilt as a capital. Its temples, palaces, and waterworks survive as one of the great ruin fields of the world, with the Virupaksha temple still in worship and the site protected by UNESCO.

title: Kashmir
summary: A Himalayan valley long called paradise on earth — celebrated for its lakes, gardens, shawls, and a syncretic culture of Hindu, Buddhist, and Sufi learning — that became the unfinished business of the 1947 Partition and one of the most militarized and bitterly disputed places on the planet, claimed in full by both India and Pakistan and partly held by China.

title: Konark and the Temple Architecture of India
summary: The Hindu temple is built less for congregation than as a built cosmos: a house for a god and a model of the universe carved in stone. Raised by the thousand across India in a shared grammar that split into magnificent regional styles, the greatest of them served as bank, school, landowner, and stage for art and dance as much as a place of worship. Konark, the thirteenth-century Sun Temple in Odisha, conceived as a colossal stone chariot of the sun-god Surya, is the tradition's boldest single statement.

title: Nalanda
summary: The vast Buddhist monastic university — a mahavihara — of eastern India that, for some seven centuries, drew students from across Asia to study philosophy, logic, medicine, and astronomy — endowed by the Gupta and Pala kings, recorded by Chinese pilgrims, and destroyed around 1200 CE in a loss that became a symbol of the end of Buddhist learning in its Indian homeland.

title: Sri Lanka
summary: The teardrop island off the southern tip of India that became, for more than two thousand years, the great fortress of Theravada Buddhism and a crossroads of the Indian Ocean — home to the ancient hydraulic civilizations of Anuradhapura and Polonnaruwa, the island where the Pali canon was first written down, ruled in turn by Sinhalese kings, south Indian invaders, and European traders, and torn in its modern life by a 26-year civil war between the Sinhalese-majority state and Tamil separatists that ended in 2009 amid mass civilian death.

title: The British East India Company
summary: The private joint-stock trading company, chartered in 1600, that ended up conquering and governing the Indian subcontinent. It began as a venture seeking spices and textiles and became a corporate state: ruling some 200 million people, fielding a private army larger than Britain's own, collecting taxes, coining money, making war and treaty, and answering ultimately to its shareholders. It financed its conquests with Indian revenue and fought them with Indian soldiers, plundered Bengal, built the Indian opium system that fed the China trade, and governed India for a century before the British Crown took it over in the wake of the Rebellion of 1857.

title: The Ganges
summary: The river that runs about 2,500 kilometers from the Himalayas across the northern plain of India and on into Bangladesh, where it joins the Brahmaputra and Meghna to reach the Bay of Bengal — worshipped by Hindus as the living goddess Ganga, depended on by hundreds of millions for water and farming, and ranked among the most polluted rivers on earth. A single channel that is at once the holiest river in Hinduism and a grave environmental crisis.

title: The Himalayas
summary: The highest mountain range on earth, raised by the collision of the Indian and Asian plates, that walls off northern India, traps the monsoon, and feeds the great rivers on which well over a billion people depend — a barrier, a water tower, and a holy landscape all at once.

title: The Taj Mahal
summary: The white marble mausoleum on the Yamuna river at Agra, built between roughly 1632 and 1653 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan for his dead wife Mumtaz Mahal — the supreme monument of Mughal architecture, a perfectly proportioned tomb set in a four-part paradise garden, and the building that became the global symbol of love and of India itself.

title: Ayurveda
period: Late Vedic roots (c. 800 BCE); classical texts last centuries BCE to early CE; living today
summary: The classical medical tradition of the Indian subcontinent, whose Sanskrit name means "the science of life" (ayus, life, and veda, knowledge). Rooted in the late Vedic world and systematized in great compendia such as the Charaka Samhita and the Sushruta Samhita, it holds that health is the balance of three bodily humors, the doshas, and treats imbalance through diet, herbs, lifestyle, oil therapies, and purification. One of the world's oldest organized medical systems and a genuine ancient achievement, it is also a booming global wellness industry whose specific therapeutic claims mostly lack robust clinical support, and some of whose traditional metal-based preparations have caused heavy-metal poisoning.

title: The Arthashastra
period: Traditionally linked to c. 300 BCE Mauryan statecraft; surviving text likely layered and redacted into the early centuries CE
summary: The great Sanskrit treatise on statecraft and political economy, traditionally attributed to Kautilya, the minister said to have helped found the Maurya Empire — a vast, coldly practical manual on how to run a state, fill its treasury, wage war, and keep a king alive through an apparatus of spies and surveillance, lost for centuries and rediscovered in the early twentieth century.

title: The Kama Sutra
period: c. 3rd century CE (near the Gupta era; dates debated)
summary: A classical Sanskrit treatise on kama, the cultured pursuit of pleasure and the art of living well, attributed to Vatsyayana Mallanaga and composed probably around the third century CE, in or near the Gupta era. Organized in seven books, it covers desire, courtship, marriage, the refined life of the city gentleman, the world of courtesans, and the emotional lives of women and lovers. The famous sexual positions occupy one part of one book. Long known in the West mainly through a loose Victorian translation by Richard Burton and Forster Arbuthnot, it is far more a guide to social and emotional life than the sex manual of popular fame.

title: Mughal Miniature Painting
summary: The court painting of the Mughal Empire: small, intensely detailed images in opaque watercolour and gold on paper, made in the imperial workshop to illustrate histories, epics, and memoirs or to fill albums. Born when the emperor Humayun brought Persian masters to India and Akbar set them to train a large workshop of mostly Indian painters, many of them Hindu, it fused Persian elegance, Indian colour and vigour, and, increasingly, European devices into one of the supreme schools of painting. It flowered under Akbar, refined under Jahangir and Shah Jahan, and dispersed to regional courts as imperial patronage faded.

title: The Ramayana
period: Older material from c. mid-first-millennium BCE; the Sanskrit epic reached its extant shape over later centuries; living tradition to the present
summary: One of the two great epics of India, a living sacred story for hundreds of millions and a cultural foundation across South and Southeast Asia: the story of the exiled prince Rama, the abduction of his wife Sita by the demon-king Ravana, and the war fought to win her back. Traditionally attributed to the sage-poet Valmiki in Sanskrit, the Ramayana is far more than one text. It exists in hundreds of tellings across languages, religions, and regions of South and Southeast Asia, which differ in their values and even in whose side they take. Its hero Rama is worshipped as an avatar of the god Vishnu, and the epic has been told, sung, painted, and performed for more than two thousand years.

title: The Rigveda
period: Composition c. 1500–1200 BCE (debated); written down many centuries later; still recited today
summary: The oldest of the four Vedas and among the oldest scriptures still in religious use, the oldest layer of Hindu sacred literature: a collection of 1,028 hymns in archaic Sanskrit, addressed to the gods of the Vedic pantheon and chanted as the liturgy of the fire sacrifice. Composed in the second millennium BCE by Indo-Aryan-speaking poets, it was preserved orally with extraordinary precision for centuries before writing, and for millennia after as a living recitation. It is the fountainhead of Hinduism and a window into a vanished world of cattle-herders and charioteers, without the temples or the great later gods of Hinduism.

title: The Upanishads
period: Principal texts c. 800–200 BCE (debated); later ones much younger; still studied and recited today
summary: The texts where Indian thought turned inward, from the fire sacrifice and the gods of the Vedas to a single radical question: what is the self, and how is it related to the ultimate reality behind everything? Composed in Sanskrit as the final layer of each Veda, the principal Upanishads (roughly a dozen, conventionally dated to about 800–200 BCE, with the count and dates both debated) are not systematic treatises but dialogues and teachings. Their most influential passages identify, or closely relate, the innermost self, atman, with Brahman, the ultimate reality behind everything — a relation later Vedanta schools read in sharply different ways. As Vedanta, "the end of the Veda," they became the seedbed of Vedanta and a great foundation of later Hindu philosophy, and the philosophical heart of Hinduism.

title: Cyclones
summary: A cyclone is what a tropical cyclone is called in the Indian Ocean and Bay of Bengal — the same storm that goes by 'hurricane' in the Atlantic and 'typhoon' in the Pacific. The physics is identical, but the geography is not. The Bay of Bengal is shallow and funnel-shaped, the delta at its head is one of the flattest and most crowded places on earth, and the storm surge has nowhere to go but over the land. The result: the deadliest tropical cyclones in human history have struck here, and the human story of South Asian cyclones is one of catastrophic mass death followed, in recent decades, by a genuine and remarkable survival revolution.

title: Caste
period: Vedic roots c. 1500–1200 BCE; caste as a developed jati order evolved over later centuries; persists today
summary: One of the world's oldest and most elaborate surviving hierarchies of birth — a system that ranked South Asian people into hereditary, endogamous, occupational groups, placed some communities outside the order altogether as 'untouchable,' and rested on ideas of ritual purity and pollution drawn from Hindu texts. Now illegal in India yet far from dead, caste has structured marriage, labor, worship, and violence for millennia, produced the Dalit liberation movement and B.R. Ambedkar's foundational challenge, and spread with the South Asian diaspora into new legal and political contests abroad.

title: Champa
summary: A Hindu kingdom of seafarers and temple-builders that endured on the central and southern coast of what is now Vietnam for more than a millennium, its last southern remnant absorbed by Vietnam in 1832. Neither Chinese nor Vietnamese, the Cham took up Indian religion, script, and statecraft, grew rich on the sea route between China and India, and left red-brick temple-towers still standing among the rice fields — yet Champa itself has nearly dropped out of the world's memory.

title: Funan
summary: One of the earliest major states of mainland Southeast Asia, and the first important Hinduized kingdom known from the region: a wealthy trading state on the Mekong delta that, around two thousand years ago, became a gateway through which Indian culture entered the region. It grew rich as a way-station on the sea route between India and China, and then faded so completely that we know it largely through Chinese reports and a scattered archaeological record, above all the port of Oc Eo. Even its name comes from Chinese records — we do not know for certain what its own people called it.

title: Majapahit
summary: Majapahit (c. 1293–1527) was the last major Hindu-Buddhist empire of the Indonesian archipelago, and one of its most powerful — a Javanese sea-power that, at its fourteenth-century height under King Hayam Wuruk and his minister Gajah Mada, claimed influence over ports and islands across maritime Southeast Asia. The entry's purpose is to explain what kind of empire a Javanese thalassocracy was, why its reported extent must be read with care, and how a vanished Hindu kingdom became modern Indonesia's imagined ancestor.

title: The Malacca Sultanate
summary: The Malacca Sultanate (c. 1400–1511) was a Malay Muslim port-kingdom on the strait that bears its name, the chokepoint between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea. It grew into one of the greatest trading emporia of maritime Southeast Asia, became a springboard for the spread of Islam across the archipelago, and fell to Portuguese guns in 1511. The entry's purpose is to explain how a small harbor became the meeting-point of the monsoon trade systems, and what it left behind for the Malay world.

title: The Dutch East Indies
period: VOC origins 1602–1799; Dutch state colony c. 1800–1949
summary: The Netherlands' colony in what is now Indonesia, born from the Dutch East India Company (the VOC) chartered in 1602 and lasting until Indonesian independence in 1949. A small European nation extracted the wealth of a vast tropical archipelago for three and a half centuries — first through what is often called the first great multinational corporation, then as a colonial state running forced-cultivation systems — and in the process drew the borders that became the modern Indonesian republic.

title: The Philippines Under Spain
summary: The 333-year period during which Spain ruled an archipelago of more than seven thousand islands in Southeast Asia — governing it from Spanish Mexico rather than Europe, knitting it to the world through a single galleon a year between Manila and Acapulco, and converting most of its lowland peoples to Roman Catholicism through the friar orders, who held vast estates and often more power than the civil government, while the Muslim south and the highlands were never fully subdued and resistance ran from Lapulapu to the revolution that ended Spanish rule.

title: The Khmer Rouge
summary: The radical Communist movement that ruled Cambodia, renamed Democratic Kampuchea, from 1975 to 1979 under Pol Pot and the secretive party known as Angkar. On taking power it emptied the cities at gunpoint, declared a new beginning it called Year Zero, abolished money, schools, religion, and private property, and drove the whole population into rural forced labor. Roughly 1.5 to 2 million Cambodians — about a quarter of the country — died from execution, starvation, overwork, and disease before a Vietnamese invasion ended the regime in 1979.

title: The Spread of Islam in Southeast Asia
summary: The gradual conversion of maritime Southeast Asia to Islam over roughly four centuries, carried not by conquest but by merchants, mystics, and marriage along the monsoon trade routes. The first firmly attested Muslim states appeared in northern Sumatra by the late thirteenth century; the faith spread through the great port of Malacca and across the archipelago as rulers converted and their subjects followed, displacing the older Hindu-Buddhist order. The process made Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei Muslim-majority and seeded Muslim communities in the southern Philippines and southern Thailand. Indonesia is today the most populous Muslim-majority country on earth.

title: Aung San
summary: The founding father of independent Burma, who led his country to the threshold of freedom from British rule and was assassinated at thirty-two, six months before independence arrived — a student radical who built the Burmese army, made a wartime bargain with Japan and then turned against it, negotiated independence with London, and tried at Panglong to bind Burma's ethnic-minority frontier peoples into a single union, leaving behind both the nation and the army that would later rule it as a dictatorship, and a counterfactual that still haunts Myanmar.

title: Ho Chi Minh
summary: The revolutionary who spent thirty years in exile organizing for Vietnamese independence, then led the movement that defeated French colonial rule and fought the United States — founding the Communist state of North Vietnam and becoming the revered 'Uncle Ho' of national legend, while his movement also built a one-party regime whose land-reform purges and suppression of rivals remain a contested part of his record. He died in 1969, six years before the 1975 reunification his cause achieved.

title: Sukarno
summary: The founding president of Indonesia, a spellbinding orator who imagined a single nation out of thousands of islands and hundreds of peoples and willed it into being. The foremost nationalist agitator against Dutch rule, jailed and exiled for it, he gave the new country a unifying creed in Pancasila, proclaimed its independence on 17 August 1945, and led it through the revolution that followed. He hosted the 1955 Bandung Conference, becoming a global voice of the non-aligned world, then abandoned parliamentary democracy for an increasingly authoritarian 'Guided Democracy' that balanced the army against the huge Communist Party. His grand balancing act collapsed after a murky 1965 coup attempt, which General Suharto used to seize power and unleash mass anti-Communist killings; Sukarno was stripped of office and died under house arrest in 1970, honored as the father of Indonesian independence and contested for the dictatorship his rule became.

title: Angkor Wat
summary: The largest religious monument on earth — a single sandstone temple in Cambodia, raised in the early twelfth century under King Suryavarman II as a scale model of the Hindu cosmos and dedicated to the god Vishnu. Built for a god and most likely for a king's own afterlife, it outlived the empire that made it, was converted from Hindu to Theravada Buddhist worship, was never fully abandoned, and became the soul and the flag of the Cambodian nation.
